name: Fabric (Python SSH Deployment & Remote Execution)
pricing_notes: Open source (BSD-2-Clause); fabric contributors / Jeff Forcier

auth.notes: SSH auth: key-based (default) or password. Reads ~/.ssh/config. connect_kwargs for custom auth.

performance.notes: Network-bound; SSH connection setup is the bottleneck

agent_readiness.notes: SSH automation and deployment for Python. from fabric import Connection. c = Connection('web1.example.com'). c.run('uname -s'). c.sudo('apt update'). c.put('myfile.conf', '/etc/myapp/'). c.get('/etc/nginx/nginx.conf'). c.local('make build'). fabfile.py: def deploy(c): c.run('git pull'). fab -H host deploy. Group execution: from fabric import SerialGroup, ThreadedGroup. group = ThreadedGroup('web1', 'web2'). group.run('systemctl restart app'). Context manager: with Connection('host') as c: ... Transfer files: c.put, c.get. Shell: c.run('cmd', warn=True) to not raise on failure. Invoke (task runner) is the task layer. v2/v3 are rewrites of classic Fabric 1.x.

gotchas: Fabric 3 is a rewrite; Fabric 1 scripts are NOT compatible
No built-in retry logic — add manually for flaky connections
sudo prompts can be tricky in non-interactive environments
ThreadedGroup uses threads, not async — GIL applies for CPU work
Fabric uses Invoke for local task running; understand the split
best_when: SSH-based deployment automation and server management
Running commands on multiple remote servers in parallel
Simple infrastructure automation without Ansible complexity
avoid_when: Complex infrastructure as code (use Ansible, Terraform, Pulumi)
summary: Fabric runs SSH commands and transfers files from Python. from fabric import Connection; c = Connection('host'); c.run('cmd'); c.sudo('cmd'); c.put('local', 'remote'); c.get('remote'). SerialGroup/ThreadedGroup for multi-host. fabfile.py + fab CLI. BSD-2. v3 is a rewrite — v1 scripts incompatible. Best for deployment scripts and SSH automation without Ansible complexity.
